segments.text: If you're waiting for the move to be obvious, you're 100% late.
You aren't the hunter, you're the exit liquidity.
The easy money has already been made.
Anyone can tell you a stock is strong when it's up 100%.
But there's a way to tell a stock is strong before it makes its move.
We call it hidden relative strength.
This is the quiet accumulation that happens
while the rest of the market is looking the other way.
It's not sexy, it won't make your X-feed, but it works.
We're going to discuss how we spot hidden relative strength,
how we measure it, and how we use it to anticipate breakouts.
It's time to become an expert on spotting hidden relative strength.
Welcome to the Trading Four podcast episode nine.
I'm Tim Belden, joined as always with Garrett Dryman
in our guide, Kurt, behind the curtain.
So Garrett, when we're saying hidden relative strength, what do we mean?
So when a lot of people talk about strength,
they're talking about a really strong stock.
And relative strength obviously is comparing one stock to another,
or one stock to the market.
Everyone knows that.
But I think that a lot of times when we talk about relative strength,
the thing that stands out to us is the strength part.
So something that has tremendous relative strength is like,
some stock that's just running, it's the strongest stock in the market,
it's super obvious.
But there are other kinds of relative strength that are more subtle.
But in our case, and you'd probably agree with this, Tim, more powerful,
because there's a way to use it to our advantage.
Now, a stock that's up 10% today, obviously,
it's probably going to have some pretty serious relative strength.
But it's like the cat's out of the bag.
So what is this hidden relative strength that we're talking about?
That is a little more subtle.
You can measure it, you can easily measure it.
But it's different than just something that's just ridiculously strong.
And what you're getting at, it's actionable.
Like it's something where you find hidden relative strength,
and that could lead to trades.
And you don't want to be looking at it every single day.
So like you said, relative strength, we compare the strength of a stock
to the indices to the market.
And for hidden relative strength, we really
want to be looking at it when the market is having down days.
When the market is weak, what's standing out and holding up the best?
And that's what gets us excited.
Yeah, absolutely.
So it's a stock that isn't going down when the market's going down.
So if we're going to compare it to the overall market,
because that's a really great proxy to say, is our stock stronger or weaker?
Then the market we can compare it to spy or cues,
or maybe it's ETF, it's sector ETF.
There's different things we can do there.
But it might be as simple as we're watching our stock.
And we use this for breakouts, mostly Tim.

It's trading in the range.
It's maybe testing the breakout level.
Maybe it's getting close, it's getting tight.
There's various reasons why we're watching it a lot closer now.

there are certain things we do below that breakout level
where we might anticipate that breakout level
or we might wait for that break.

is, relatives trying.

because I brought a couple slides of an indicator
that I created that I like to use to measure this,
especially on the quant side.
Let's go.
But it's usually as simple as, like, I'm just watching the stock.
And you notice that the market ticks down.

something that, like, something's there.
There's a bid there.

What is green?

saying the underlying bid, what I find so
interesting with hidden relative strength,
is that there is a fundamental driver behind it,
where the strongest stocks, usually they make
the biggest, most explosive moves
because they have institutional buying behind it.

you can't do it in one single day.
So on those down market days, you're

And that's kind of the dynamic that is that underlying bid
that we're talking about.
Right, it's like the footprints of those institutions.

The market having those down moves
gives you an opportunity to see which stocks have bids in them.

I think the traditional way to do it
is like a ratio chart,
where you're kind of like dividing like one stock by the other

Right? And the movement of that line

like that's a great way to do it.

it doesn't really normalize for volatility.

So like for instance, if Tesla moves a lot more than the market,
so the market's up 2%, Tesla's up 4%,
markets down 1%, Tesla's down 2%, right?
It's just Tesla's doing the same thing as the market.
It's just more volatile, right?
In a situation like that,
a lot of these traditional relative strength lines
are gonna end up just looking like the chart of Tesla,
like a sort of subdued chart of Tesla,
because Tesla's gonna dominate
because it has more volatility than the instrument
